Open January 2017.

(Positions available!)

Research

Neglected Diseases (NTDs) caused by parasitic worms (helminths) impose a debilitating health and economic burden throughout much of the world. These global "diseases of poverty" infect over 1.5 billion humans and exert their damage through a wide range of species-specific clinical manifestations. Parasitic diseases also constitute a major challenge to animal and plant health. The central ambition of our laboratory is to combine advanced molecular, genetics, and computational approaches to make discoveries that improve our understanding of parasite biology and our ability to diagnose and treat the infections they cause. This includes the identification and validation of new targets for drug discovery, the elucidation of new mechanisms of drug (anthelmintic) resistance, and the development of new tools for parasite manipulation. Our initial work will rely on the direct study of vector-borne parasites (Brugia malayi) and the leveraging of powerful model organism systems (Caenorhabditis elegans). We strive to place our work in the context of a growing appreciation and understanding of parasite genetic diversity. Detailed summaries of funded research projects will be outlined on this page.

Past and Present Funding Sources

↓ Past (pre-Wisconsin) publications

2016 Zamanian M and Andersen EC. Prospects and challenges of CRISPR/Cas genome editing for the study and control of neglected vector‐borne nematode diseases. FEBS Journal. (CRISPR/Cas9 gene editing special issue)

2015 Zamanian M*, Fraser L*, Agbedanu P, Harischandra H, Day TA, Bartholomay L, and Kimber MJ. Release of Small RNA-containing exosomes from the human filarial parasite Brugia malayi. PLoS Negl Trop Dis. (*Equal contribution)

2015 Chan JD, Agbedanu PN, Grab T, Zamanian M, Yang Y, Li F, Dosa PI, Day TA and Marchant JS. Ergot alkaloids (re)generate new leads as antiparasitics. PLoS Negl Trop Dis.

2015 Zamanighomi M*, Zamanian M*, Kimber M, Wang Z. Gene regulatory network inference from perturbed time-series expression data via ordered dynamical expansion of non-steady state actors. IEEE ACM Transactions on Computational Biology and Bioinformatics. (*Equal contribution)

2015 Wheeler NW, Kimber MJ, Ribero P, Day TA, and Zamanian M. Functional analysis of Girardia tigrina transcriptome seeds pipeline for antischistosomal discovery. Parasites & Vectors 2015. (Highly Accessed)

2014 Chan JD, Agbedanu PN, Zamanian M, Gruba SM, Haynes CL, Day TA, and Marchant JS. ‘Death and axes’: unexpected Ca2+ entry phenologs predict new anti-schistosomal agents. PLoS Pathogens.

2014 Ciaran M, Atkinson LE, Zamanian M, McVeigh P, Day TA, Kimber MJ, Marks NJ, Maule AG, Mousley A. New insights into the FLPergic complements of parasitic nematodes: Informing deorphanisation approaches. EuPA Open Proteomics.

2013 Brewer MT, Agbedanu PN, Zamanian M, Day TA, Carlson S. Evidence for a Bacterial Lipopolysaccharide-recognizing G protein-coupled Receptor in the Bacterial Engulfment by Entamoeba histolytica. Eukaryotic Cell.

2013 Tsai IJ, Zarowiecki M, Holroyd N, Garciarrubio A, Sanchez-Flores A, Brooks KL, Tracey A, Bobes RJ, Fragoso G, Sciutto E, Aslett M, Beasley H, Cai J, Camicia F, Clark R, Cucher M, De Silva N, Day TA, Deplazes P, Estrada K, Fernandez C, Holland PWH, Hou J, Hu S, Huckvale T, Hung SS, Kamenetzky L, Keane JA, Kiss F, Koziol U, Lambert O, Liu K, Luo X, Luo Y, Macchiaroli N, Nichol S, Paps J, Parkinson J, Stantcheva NP, Riddiford N, Rosenzvit M, Salinas G, Wasmuth JD, Zamanian M, Zheng Y, Cai X, Soberón X, Olson PD, Laclette JP, Brehm K, Berriman M. The genomes of four tapeworm species reveal adaptations to parasitism. Nature.

2013 Atkinson L, Stevenson M, McCoy C, Maule AG, Marks NJ, Fleming C, Zamanian M, Day TA, Kimber MJ, Mousley A. flp-32 ligand/receptor silencing phenocopy a faster phenotype in plant pathogenic nematodes. PLoS Pathogens.

2012 Zamanian M, Agbedanu PN, Wheeler NW, Kimber MJ, Day TA. Novel RNAi-mediated approach to G protein-coupled receptor deorphanization: proof of principle and characterization of a planarian 5-HT receptor. PLoS One.

2011 Zamanian M, Kimber MJ, McVeigh P, Carlson SA, Maule AG, Day TA. The repertoire of G protein-coupled receptors in the human parasite Schistosoma mansoni and the model organism Schmidtea mediterranea. BMC Genomics. (Highly Accessed)

2011 McVeigh P, Mair GR, Novozhilova E, Day A, Zamanian M, Marks NJ, Kimber MJ, Day TA, Maule AG. Schistosome I/Lamides - A new family of bioactive helminth neuropeptides. Int J Parasitol.

2010 Novozhilova E, Kimber MJ, Qian H, McVeigh P, Robertson AP, Zamanian M, Maule AG, Day TA. FMRFamide-like peptides (FLPs) enhance voltage-gated calcium currents to elicit muscle contraction in the human parasite Schistosoma mansoni. PLoS Negl Trop Dis.

2009 Berriman M, Haas BJ, LoVerde PT, Wilson RA, Dillon GP, Cerqueira GC, Mashiyama ST, Al-Lazikani B, Andrade LF, Ashton PD, Aslett MA, Bartholomeu DC, Blandin G, Caffrey CR, Coghlan A, Coulson R, Day TA, Delcher A, DeMarco R, Djikeng A, Eyre T, Gamble JA, Ghedin E, Gu Y, Hertz-Fowler C, Hirai H, Hirai Y, Houston R, Ivens A, Johnston DA, Lacerda D, Macedo CD, McVeigh P, Ning Z, Oliveira G, Overington JP, Parkhill J, Pertea M, Pierce RJ, Protasio AV, Quail MA, Rajandream MA, Rogers J, Sajid M, Salzberg SL, Stanke M, Tivey AR, White O, Williams DL, Wortman J, Wu W, Zamanian M, Zerlotini A, Fraser-Liggett CM, Barrell BG, El-Sayed NM. The Genome of the blood fluke Schistosoma mansoni. Nature. (Cover Article)

2009 Kimber MJ, Sayegh L, El-Shehabi F, Song C, Zamanian M, Woods D, Day TA, Ribeiro P. Identification of an Ascaris G protein-coupled acetylcholine receptor with atypical muscarinic pharmacology. Int J Parasitol.

2009 McVeigh P, Mair G, Atkinson L, Ladurner P, Zamanian M, Novozhilova E, Marks N, Day TA, Maule AG. Discovery of multiple neuropeptide families in the phylum Platyhelminthes. Int J Parasitol.

Tools

In the interests of open science and collaboration, all raw data and computational pipelines associated with lab preprints and published manuscripts will be made available through GitHub and other public servers.

ZamanianLab Github

Contact

Hiring: We are actively looking to recruit highly motivated undergraduates, research specialists, graduate students, and postdocs who share our scientific curiosity and interests. Prospective undergraduates, research specialists, and postdocs should contact the PI directly. Prospective graduate students should gain admission to one of the graduate programs for which the PI serves as a trainer.

PI E-mail: mzamanian at wisc.edu

Address:
PI Office: Room 205
Main Lab: Room 230
Hanson Biomedical Sciences Building
University of Wisconsin-Madison
1656 Linden Drive
Madison, WI 53706-1581
